title: Crystallography of periodic nanotextures in a strained Mott insulator
authors: Benjamin Z. Gregory, Yorick A. Birkhölzer, Noah Schnitzer, Ziming Shao, Jeff Hodgson, Suchismita Sarker, Jacob P. Ruff, Berit H. Goodge, David A. Muller, Kyle M. Shen, Darrell G. Schlom, Andrej Singer
abstract: Here we investigate stripes of alternating structural phases spontaneously forming in epitaxially strained $Ca_2RuO_4$ thin films below the metal-insulator transition. Using large-volume X-ray reciprocal-space mapping, we show that satellite-pattern intensities across 24 symmetry-inequivalent Bragg reflections collapse onto a single parameter-free curve. The collapse identifies a coherent martensitic laminate of few-nm-wide domains separated by ${012}$ interfaces, with displacements along $\left\langle01\bar{2}\right\rangle$. Satellite-extinction analysis demonstrates that both coexisting phases retain the bulk orthorhombic space group despite the pseudocubic $LaAlO_3$ substrate, biaxial epitaxial strain, and intrinsic strain at the interfaces. Classical invariant-plane-strain crystallography thus governs the nanoscale domain geometry of a Mott insulator with intertwined magnetic, electronic, and lattice order.
summary: This work uses advanced X-ray diffraction techniques to study strain-induced stripe domains in a Mott insulator film. By applying classical invariant-plane-strain crystallography theory, the authors successfully model and predict the observed satellite scattering patterns. This confirms that the nanoscale domain structure is fundamentally governed by lattice compatibility rather than solely electronic interactions.
main_problem: The study investigates the spontaneous formation of alternating structural phases (stripes) in epitaxially strained Ca2RuO4 thin films below a metal-insulator transition, and determines the crystallographic principles governing these domain patterns.
main_result: Satellite-pattern intensities collapse onto a single parameter-free curve predicted by classical invariant-plane-strain crystallography, confirming the existence of a coherent martensitic laminate structure.
method: The research combines large-volume X-ray reciprocal-space mapping with theoretical modeling based on Invariant Plane Strain (IPS) deformation theory to analyze diffraction patterns.
model_or_system: The physical system is epitaxially strained Ca2RuO4 thin films, a Mott insulator undergoing structural transitions. The analysis models the domain geometry using classical invariant-plane-strain crystallography, which dictates how coherent interfaces accommodate transformation strain.
key_observables: Satellite-pattern intensities across multiple Bragg reflections; Symmetry Quotient (SQ'') quantifying relative satellite streak brightness; shifts in reciprocal lattice vectors ($\Delta\mathbf{G}$).
important_parameters: Temperature (below metal-insulator transition); Strain magnitude; Domain width (few-nm); Crystallographic symmetry group (Pbca).
main_assumptions: The domain geometry is governed by Classical invariant-plane-strain crystallography, and the analysis assumes epitaxial strain enforces a compatible plane templating the periodic laminate structure.
figures_summary: Figures show parameter-free collapse of satellite intensities onto IPS predictions; diagrams illustrate interface shifts ($\Delta\mathbf{G}$); STEM images confirm coherent interfaces between L and S phases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the structural transition and domain formation via X-ray mapping, developing the theoretical framework using invariant plane strain crystallography to model the satellite intensity dependence, and finally confirming the model's predictive power across multiple reflections.
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state structure, the analysis of phase transitions, lattice coupling, and symmetry breaking in a correlated material like RuO4 provides insights into how electronic correlations manifest through structural degrees of freedom, relevant to understanding emergent quantum phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Striped nanotexture and reciprocal space mapping of Ca2RuO4. (a) Resistivity of the strained Ca2RuO4 thin film showing a metal-insulator transition at 200 K. The film is homogeneous at high temperatures and forms a periodic nanotexture of distinct structural domains resembling the bulk L-Pbca and S-Pbca phases (red and black stripes). (Inset) Ca2RuO4 unit cell (Ca: gray, O: red, Ru: blue). (b) Real space: atomic positions in the two structural variants, with interface normal 𝒏 and the displacements 𝒖 all parallel to 𝐥. Reciprocal space: The reciprocal lattice points of the distorted lattice are shifted by 𝚫𝐆 along 𝐧. The lattice remains undistorted along the line through the...

Figure 2: Parameter-free collapse of satellite intensities onto the invariant-plane-strain prediction. (a) Magnified reciprocal space maps around selected reciprocal lattice points. The schematics under each panel show which interface variant contributes to the satellite pattern at that reflection (interface along (012) (blue), or along (01'2) (red), or both). (b) Reciprocal-space schematic showing the contributions of both interface variants. Symbols denote the two coexisting structures (S-Pbca, no edge color; L-Pbca, black edge). Dashed lines are normal to the displacement direction for each interface variant. (c) Symmetry quotient measured at 24 symmetry-inequivalent reflections with...

Figure 3: Crystallography of coexisting domains. (a) Crystal truncation rods extracted from the 3D reciprocal-space map of Ca2RuO4 at 50 K, taken along l at four in-plane reciprocal-lattice positions hk = 02, 20, 01, and 10; asterisks mark peaks from the LaAlO3 substrate. At 300 K, the peak intensities are similar, and satellites are absent (Fig. S4). (b) Satellite patterns around the indicated reflections, shown in slices through reciprocal space at fixed l. Unprimed indices label the primary orthorhombic twin; primed indices label the 90°-rotated twin.
